items.title: AP x-ray in hip dislocation
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis by simple AP radiography

  • Key finding in posterior dislocation: the femoral head is displaced superolaterally, with the femur in adduction and internal rotation; the head appears small compared to the contralateral acetabulum (small head sign).
  • Key finding in anterior dislocation: the femoral head appears large and superimposed on the acetabulum, with the femur in abduction and external rotation.
  • Evaluate the joint space: if it is increased suggests osteochondral fragment or soft tissue interposition.
  • Look for associated fractures: of the posterior acetabular rim, femoral head (Pipkin) or fracture of the ipsilateral femoral shaft (high-energy traumatic injury).

items.title: Ludloff sign
items.contentHtml:
Ludloff sign in posterior dislocation

  • Ludloff sign: on the anteroposterior hip x-ray, the posteriorly dislocated femoral head projects above the Shenton line (arc formed by the superior edge of the obturator foramen and the medial edge of the femoral neck).
  • The interruption of the line of Shenton indicates cephalic displacement of the femoral head.
  • It is a pathognomonic sign of posterior hip dislocation on the simple anteroposterior radiograph.
  • It complements the small head sign and helps differentiate posterior from anterior dislocation.

items.title: Differences between anterior and posterior dislocation
items.contentHtml:
Anterior vs. posterior dislocation

  • Posterior dislocation (>90% of dislocations): mechanism of impact on flexed knee (car dashboard); the femur is in adduction and internal rotation; the head is small on anteroposterior radiograph; risk of injury to the sciatic nerve.
  • Anterior dislocation (<10%): mechanism of fall with hip in abduction and external rotation; the head is large on anteroposterior radiograph; risk of femoral nerve injury and vascular compromise (femoral artery).
  • Classification of Thompson-Epstein (posterior) and Epstein (anterior) based on the presence of associated fractures.

items.title: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
items.contentHtml:
Most feared complication: avascular necrosis

  • Avascular necrosis of the femoral head is the most serious complication, secondary to the interruption of the flow of the medial femoral circumflex artery (branch of the deep branch of the medial femoral artery).
  • The incidence increases with the time of dislocation: <6 hours the rate of avascular necrosis is <5%; >12 hours exceeds 30%.
  • Urgent closed reduction (<6 hours from trauma) is the most effective preventive measure.
  • Early post-reduction diagnosis is made with magnetic resonance imaging at 3-6 months (sensitivity >90%).
  • Treatment of established avascular necrosis includes spinal cord decompression or total hip arthroplasty according to stage.

items.title: Urgent closed reduction
items.contentHtml:
Urgent closed reduction (<6 hours)

  • The closed reduction should be performed less than 6 hours from the dislocation to minimize the risk of avascular necrosis of the femoral head.
  • It is performed under intravenous sedation or general anesthesia to obtain complete muscle relaxation.
  • Before reduction, document the distal neurovascular status (especially function of the sciatic nerve: plantar and dorsiflexion of the foot, sensitivity in the lateral edge of the leg and foot).
  • Post-reduction: confirm the reduction with anteroposterior radiography and evaluate articular congruity; If there is asymmetry of the joint space (>2 mm), suspect interposition of bone fragment or soft tissues.

items.title: Allis maneuver
items.contentHtml:
Reduction technique: Allis maneuver

  • Allis maneuver: closed reduction technique for posterior hip dislocation.
  • Patient in supine position; the assistant stabilizes the pelvis by pressing the anterior superior iliac spines against the table.
  • The operator flexes the hip and knee of the affected limb to 90°, applies axial traction on the axis of the femur, combining gentle adduction and internal rotation to unlock the femoral head from the posterior acetabular edge.
  • Alternatives: Stimson maneuver (patient in recumbency). prone with vertical traction) and Bigelow maneuver (traction with external rotation and abduction).
  • The Allis maneuver is the most used due to its effectiveness and lower risk of iatrogenic fracture.

items.title: Femoral head fracture (Pipkin)
items.contentHtml:
Associated fracture of the femoral head

  • The fracture of the femoral head associated with posterior dislocation is classified according to Pipkin:
  • Pipkin I: fracture below the fovea (does not compromise the bearing surface).
  • Pipkin II: fracture above the fovea (compromises the bearing surface). load).
  • Pipkin III: fracture of the femoral head with fracture of the femoral neck.
  • Pipkin IV: fracture of the femoral head with fracture of the acetabular rim.
  • Treatment of Pipkin I and II with small fragment (<1 cm) is closed reduction and follow-up; Large or displaced fragments require open reduction and internal fixation (Herbert or compression screws).

items.foco: Sciatic nerve injury in posterior dislocation
items.title: Sciatic nerve injury
items.contentHtml:
Injury of the sciatic nerve in posterior dislocation

  • The injury of the sciatic nerve occurs in 10-15% of posterior hip dislocations, due to stretching or direct compression of the nerve against the posterior acetabular edge.
  • The common peroneal branch of the sciatic nerve is the most affected (due to its fixation in the fibular neck), clinically it produces foot drop (deficit in ankle dorsiflexion and extension of the fingers) and hypoesthesia in the dorsum of the foot and lateral border of the leg.
  • The neurological examination should be performed before and after the reduction.
  • Most lesions are neuroapraxias (partial axonotmesis) and recover in weeks to months; complete recovery occurs in 60-70% of cases.
  • If the neurological deficit appears after reduction, suspect entrapment or compression by a bone fragment; indicate urgent surgical exploration.

items.title: Post-reduction immobilization with traction
items.contentHtml:
Post-reduction immobilization with traction

  • After successful closed reduction, cutaneous traction (or skeletal if there is an unstable associated fracture) is indicated for 2 to 4 weeks to keep the femoral head centered and allow capsular healing.
  • The cutaneous traction is applied with a weight of 2-4 kg, with the hip in slight flexion. (20-30°) and abduction (10-15°).
  • In simple dislocations (without associated fracture), traction is maintained for 2-3 weeks, followed by partial unloading with crutches for 6-8 weeks.
  • In fracture-dislocations (Pipkin or acetabular fracture), traction can be prolonged depending on the stability of the fracture.
  • Objective: prevent reluxation and avascular necrosis by stabilizing the capsular vascular supply.

items.title: Open reduction indication
items.contentHtml:
Indication of open reduction

  • Open reduction indicated when closed reduction fails (impossible to achieve concentric reduction) or when there is articular incongruity post-reduction (>2 mm of asymmetry of the joint space in anteroposterior and oblique Yudet radiograph).
  • Causes of failure: soft tissue interposition (capsule, labrum, obturator internus tendon) or osteochondral fragment trapped in the joint.
  • It is also indicated in Pipkin II-IV fractures with significant displacement (>2 mm) or large fragment (>1 cm) that compromises the load-bearing surface.
  • The most common approach is the posterior (Kocher-Langenbeck) approach. for posterior dislocations; the anterior (Smith-Petersen) for anterior dislocations.

items.title: Residual instability assessment
items.contentHtml:
Evaluation of residual instability

  • After closed or open reduction, residual instability is evaluated by stress maneuver under fluoroscopy or in the operating room.
  • With the patient sedated, forced internal rotation and adduction of the hip are performed; if the femoral head is subluxated posteriorly (>3 mm displacement), the instability is significant.
  • The instability may be due to posterior acetabular defect (rim fracture) or capsulolabral injury.
  • If the instability is >5 mm or reluxation occurs, surgical repair is required (capsulorrhaphy, osteosynthesis of the acetabular rim or labral reconstruction).
  • Post-reduction computed tomography is useful to quantify the acetabular bone defect and plan surgery.

items.title: Follow-up with MRI for avascular necrosis
items.contentHtml:
MRI follow-up for avascular necrosis

  • MRI is the method of choice for the early diagnosis of avascular necrosis of the post-dislocation femoral head.
  • It is recommended to perform MRI at 3 months after reduction, and repeat it at 6 and 12 months if there are risk factors. risk (dislocation time >6 hours, femoral neck fracture, use of corticosteroids).
  • Early findings in MRI: low signal band in T1 and T2 sequences in the subchondral region (double line sign in T2: internal line of high signal and external line of low signal).
  • The classification of Ficat and Arlet (stages I-IV) is based on magnetic resonance imaging and plain radiography findings; Stage I (only changes on MRI) is reversible if treated early.
  • Bone scintigraphy with technetium-99m can show a cold area in the early stages, but is less sensitive than MRI.
